data.title: Caleres: A Surprising And Interesting Move For Stuart Weitzman
data.description:
Shares of Caleres, Inc. (NYSE:CAL) saw a relief move higher in February as the company announced a surprising deal after quite a tough period after the company had announced multiple guidance cuts in a row.

A dirt cheap valuation and modest leverage, in combination with a surprising and somewhat interesting deal, make me intrigued regarding the prospects of the pro forma business going forward, as I look forward to learning more about the impact of the deal in the coming months.

A Shoe Business

Caleres, founded in 1878, describes itself as a merchant-driven shoe organization with powerful brand assets, claiming expertise in sourcing, logistics, marketing analytics and technology.

A unique and synergistic structure, passionate and ambitious teams support strong brand names like Vionic, Allen Edmonds, Franco Sarto, Vince, LifeStride and Blowfish Malibu, to name a few.

In recent years the company has been on a stable footing (no pun intended) with sales having ranged between $2.8 and $3 billion in the period 2021-2023, as adjusted operating margins around 7% of sales allowed for earnings between $4.00 and $4.50 per share.

Zooming into the 2023 results, Caleres reported a 5% fall in annual sales to $2.82 billion as operating profits were down nearly a tenth to $194 million. GAAP earnings were down $10 million to $171 million, with GAAP earnings reported at $4.80 per share. These earnings were positively impacted by a deferred tax valuation allowance adjustment, with adjusted earnings reported at $4.18 per share.

These results were reported across two segments. The largest of these is a $1.61 billion Famous Footwear segment. Revenues in this segment were down over 5%, as the company managed to post segment margins at 7% and change, based on a share count of 860 stores by year-end.

The Brand Portfolio is a bit smaller with $1.27 billion in sales, but with just 98 stores, these stores on average are far more productive, also visible given the fact that operating margins come in at 11% and change.

For 2024, the company guided for flattish sales, with revenues seen between flat and positive 2%, and adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $4.30-$4.60 per share.

The Valuation Game

The 35 million shares of Caleres traded around the $40 mark early in 2024, grating equity in the business to a $1.4 billion valuation. This excluded a $161 million net debt load at the time (excluding lease assets and liabilities), with leverage being reasonable, as a $40 stock traded at 9-10 times adjusted earnings multiple.

After shares peaked at $45 in September of last year, shares have gradually come down to current levels of just $17. This was driven by three sequential cuts in the full-year outlook since September, with tough market conditions resulting in the lowered guidance. This calls for 2024 sales to be down 3.0-3.5%, but moreover, adjusted earnings are now seen at a midpoint of $3.25 per share.

Down to $17 per share, the 34 million shares grant equity of the business a mere $578 million valuation. This excludes a $204 million net debt load (which excludes lease liabilities and assets) for a $780 million enterprise valuation. With revenues seen close to $2.7 billion, the business trades at just a quarter of sales, with equity trading at just 5 times earnings here. With trailing adjusted EBITDA trending at $237 million, leverage ratios come in below 1 times, all looking quite reasonable.

A Big Deal

Amidst the difficulties of the own business, Caleres announced a surprising deal in February. The timing was of interest given the challenges faced by the business, but it was also the asset and the seller which surprised me.

Caleres announced a $105 million cash deal to acquire Stuart Weitzman from Tapestry, Inc. (TPR). At first sight, the deal looks like a bargain, as Tapestry bought the business nearly a decade ago at a price tag of over half a billion.

In its annual report, Tapestry announced a $241 million revenue contribution from Stuart Weitzman, although revenues were down substantially and the unit reported $21 million in segment losses.

Pro forma, the deal will boost sales by nearly 10%, yet Stuart Weitzman is posting losses. On the other hand, it might be of better allure, once and if the business is turned around. This ignites real potential for Caleres, but it will result in leverage increasing in absolute terms, but certainly in relative terms given the incurred losses which pressure near-term EBITDA while debt levels will go up.

Shares of Caleres moved up 5% in response to the deal, but this is just a $30 million move in reaction to a $105 million deal. Frankly, the near-term economics do not look too compelling, but the potential is there, but for that, one has to believe in management's ability to deliver on turning the brand around.

What Now?

Given the discussion above, I have mixed feelings about this deal. I see the near-term uncertainty, but also the long-term potential. For that, I look forward to more clues about the deal and the pro forma impact, due to be provided in March when the annual results are formally released.

For now, I'm appealed to the dirt cheap valuation and the intriguing deal, but not yet willing to commit as the business has seen a decade-long period of stagnation. However, I look forward to updating the thesis in March as more becomes known.

data.title: Alibaba Earnings Make It Official; Why I'm Buying China
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) just reported Q4 earnings, crushing expectations and soaring over 7% after the announcement. The company has seen encouraging dynamics in profitability and growth in Cloud, and also seems to be capturing upside from AI.

The outlook for BABA seems quite positive, and this comes at a time when it looks like the broader Chinese market may be on its way to recovery.

If China is once again investable, which I make a case here that it is, then BABA is one of the best stocks to gain exposure.

Based on past valuation metrics, the stock could still double from here.

BABA Q3 Earnings

Alibaba just reported a solid Q3 earnings bear, and the stock is up on the news.

Alibaba’s revenues came in at $38.4 billion, an 8% YoY increase. Taobao and Tmall increased 9%, Cloud revenues 13% and Digital Commerce leapfrogged 32%.

On the profitability side, we saw the company’s margins boosted through operational efficiency.

Adjusted EBITDA increased by 4% to $7.5 billion and net income increased 333% to $6.4 billion.

Alibaba also repurchased 119 million shares for over $1.3 billion and issued $55 billion in senior notes. The company also sold its stake in Sun Art and Intimate, worth a total combined of $2.6 billion

What’s Next For BABA?

After stellar earnings, Alibaba has attracted investors once more. The company is performing well and also has a bright outlook.

Like many of its tech peers in America, BABA is also focusing on AI in order to drive growth and profitability.

Cloud holds a lot of potential, and the company believes its AI products will sustain triple-digit.

On the e-commerce side, BABA is looking to improve customer loyalty through the expansion of its 88VIP benefits and is also expanding into other markets, recently South Korea, through a strategic JV.

On the financial side, it seems that the company is committed to financial discipline, focusing on its core business through divestitures and giving back to investors through continued share repurchases.

BABA has given investors a lot to be happy about after earnings, but let’s not forget that the stock has been rallying for the last month, up over 60% since recent lows.

And this is a trend we have observed in a more general sense.

Is China Investable Again?

The reception of these latest BABA earnings shows a clear change in sentiment in China, and this is also supported by market technicals.

The chart above shows the market breadth for the Hang Seng Index. As we can see, whenever this breaks above 60, we see a rally in Chinese stocks. These have been short in the past few instances, but this time could signal the beginning of a longer bull market.

The MSCI China ETF has also broken out of a falling wedge and is showing very strong momentum.

Challenges remain for 2025, and President Xi Jinping recently hosted a summit with business leaders in order to address some of the issues plaguing the Chinese economy.

But could the worst now be priced in?

How High Could BABA Go?

For a long time, BABA has faced two challenges.

Firstly, in the last few quarters, investors have doubted the company’s profitability and ability to pay back investors.

Secondly, BABA has suffered from the Chinese discount, as these stocks always trade at a discount compared to US stocks.

However, both these dark clouds are slowly lifting, and we could soon see BABA trade back near previous levels.

According to Gurufocus, BABA’s media PEG has been around 1.15. It now trades at 0.79 forward PE. If this ratio was closer to 1.15, then we'd see 40% upside from here.

This makes sense since it's still well below the sector median of 1.45. BABA would still trade at a discount, just not such a high discount.

Technical Analysis

40% upside from here would get us well into a key resistance level above, which sits around the $170-$190 region.

This level also lines up well with the 1.618 ext of the three-point fib extension from the recent high and low.

We’ve now broken above that January high, and we don’t have much resistance until we reach that zone above.

Risks

Nonetheless, there's still substantial risk when it comes to investing in BABA, perhaps now more than ever.

The new administration has made it very clear that it wants to impose global tariffs. So far, the market has digested these well. A lot of people seem to believe that tariffs are simply being used as bargaining chips, but things could still very much escalate from here.

Tariffs would hurt both China and the US in terms of GDP, which would impact BABA, and they could of course dampen investor sentiment, leading to an even larger discount on Chinese stocks

Final Thoughts

These latest earnings and the investor reaction have made it clear that investors are once again comfortable with Chinese stocks. BABA is perhaps the best way to gain exposure, with a strong business and bright future outlook.

data.title: President Trump And The CFPB
data.description:
The CFPB (Consumer Financial Protection Bureau)

In a recent Biltmore View podcast (click here to listen), we explored the shifting regulatory landscape, particularly regarding digital currencies and the CFPB’s uncertain future under a second Trump administration.

It didn’t take long for President Trump to alter the CFPB’s agenda. Established in 2010 as part of the Dodd-Frank Act, a sweeping financial reform bill spanning over 10,000 pages, the CFPB was created to protect consumers from risky lending practices that contributed to the 2008 financial crisis. Since the CFPB was created by Congress, the Trump administration cannot unilaterally eliminate it. However, a shift in leadership quickly reshaped its direction.

First, the White House nominated Jonathan McKernan, a former FDIC board member, as the full-time director. Shortly after, on February 7, President Trump appointed Russell Vought as acting director. Vought, who also serves as President Trump’s budget director, is widely seen as one of the architects of Project 2025. Published by the Heritage Foundation, this report calls for the CFPB's abolition.

In his first memo to CFPB employees, Vought suspended nearly all staff activities, halted new funding, and closed the headquarters for a week. He then issued sweeping directives that effectively froze the agency's operations, including:

  • Do not approve or issue any proposed or final rules or formal or informal guidance
  • Suspend the effective dates of all final rules that have been issued
  • Suspend any final rules that have not yet become effective
  • Not commence any investigative activities and do not open any new investigations
  • Do not settle any current or pending enforcement actions
  • Issue no public communications (i.e., publication of research papers or compliance bulletins)
  • Cease all supervision and examination activity
  • Cease all stakeholder engagement

This abrupt policy shift marks a stark contrast to the aggressive regulatory approach of former Director Rohit Chopra. While few financial institutions will mourn the CFPB’s dismantling, its sudden shutdown is already creating ripple effects across consumer finance.

Mortgage companies, auto and payday lenders, payment apps, and many FINTECH firms are now essentially unsupervised. With the CFPB effectively sidelined, consumers who experience unfair treatment will find no federal agency to turn to for recourse. Some are concerned that without an agency like the CFPB “on the watch”, there is a large regulatory void that could leave Americans vulnerable to predatory practices. Fed Chairman Jerome Powell recently warned Congress that, with the CFPB incapacitated, no other federal regulator is currently enforcing key consumer finance laws.

The CFPB had some good consumer protection rules, especially when it came to areas like payday lenders and certain specialty finance areas. However, some claimed that the agency tended to overstep its authority, pursue aggressive and overzealous enforcement actions against financial firms.

The industry’s mixed feelings of relief and concern underscore how the Trump administration’s sweeping remake of the federal government is likely to lead to consequences that are not fully understood. While state attorneys general may attempt to fill this regulatory void, the long-term impact remains uncertain. Whether this shift fosters financial innovation or exposes consumers to new risks is still an open question.

data.title: QQH: Look Beyond The Marketing, This ETF Has Big Flaws
data.description:
Investors are subjected to so much hype and bravado, they can be excused for missing some of the important "due diligence" points that are not always apparent. I am no different, and I get fooled too. But hopefully after 30 years of ETF, stock and macro market analysis, I've reached the point where my mantra of "avoid big loss" remains intact.

Beware the bull market in "shiny objects"

This is a market environment characterized by amazing tales of investing styles that seem too good to be true. Most are. That's not a cynical view, because no one can blame ETF companies for trying. Among the "alternative" investment approaches I find most overhyped compared to their actual ability to avoid major losses are so-called "buffered" ETFs. These cushion the initial decline in bear markets, but leave the door open for significant asset bleeding. They are not the subject of this article, and I'll take my shot at some of them in the coming weeks.

The topic here is Howard Capital's HCM Defender 100 Index ETF (NYSEARCA:QQH), which is similar to a mutual fund that preceded it by several years, dating back to 2014. That allows us to judge it, not in a sustained bear market (since we haven't had one during that time) but over some shorter bursts where it went lower, including those in late 2018, early 2020 and the first 9 months of 2022.

The idea of QQH is straightforward: separate time into 2 separate conditions. Those when the Nasdaq 100 is going up and those when it is going down. The flatter periods are, as I see it, grouped with the up periods. The fund's 2 main weapons are Nasdaq-oriented ETFs and stocks, and T-bills or similar cash equivalents. This is a true "market timing" strategy.

What is "market timing" anyway?

Marketing timing is dirty word in some investing circles. Ironically, it is dismissed by some of the very people who practice it. They just don't consider what they do to be market timing. I'm talking about the "quarterly rebalancing" crowd, as in owning an allegedly diversified mix of stock and bond asset styles, and at the same time each calendar quarter, selling down the winners and buying more of the losers to reset each asset class to its "model weight." This is part science, part marketing scheme and part attempt to keep fee-based assets from fleeing, under the guise of "Nobel Prize-winning" investing theory, which is now nearly 50 years old and doesn't work at all like it used to. But I digress.

Marketing timing is part of investing. That is, whatever investment one is making, the time at which the investment process is executed is determined and plotted. Even if it is semi-automated.

With QQH, I have zero issue with the attempt to shift from stocks to cash or vice versa. All I care about is if it works. To some, "works" means that it gets its fair share of the up cycles, and really clamps down on rough markets. Somewhere along the way, losing 20% of one's peak portfolio value became acceptable. Not to me, and I assume not to my subscribers.

Defend? Against what?

QQH's timing mechanism, like the mutual fund version that preceded it, has a mixed record so far. I would expect to see regular cases of limiting losses that separate the ETF from QQQ. Nope. Sometimes it defends as the name says, sometimes it doesn't.

Over time, QQH has done quite well, nearly equaling QQQ the past 5 years. However, this is not a period in which the hedging mechanism was truly stress tested in terms of a long-lived decline. And when sudden market breaks did occur, it gave up plenty of territory.

Drilling down, and using the mutual fund version to go back 10 years instead of 5, QQH has lost 20% or close to that on several occasions...in just 3 months' time! As a career risk manager, who was OK with not making 30-60% in a single year in exchange for "avoiding big loss" as I defined it, this is outside the lines for me.

And below, there is obviously a mechanism at work to stem some of the worst losses. But that purple line exceeds 20% too often, and in 2022 this strategy lost about 40%, which was notably more than QQQ.

I'm all for taking emotion out of the equation, as this graphic from the firm's website states. But a 40% loss from peak to trough indicates there is some whipsaw impact on the "secret sauce" here, known as the HCM-Buy-Line.

Despite this, and maybe due to the fact that Howard Capital has an investment advisory firm, whose client base may account for a chunk of that $586mm in assets, QQH is thriving from a business standpoint. A 1% expense ratio on that AUM, do the math, that's good living!

The holdings' allocation by sector below indicates that QQQ-like focus, and the table below it shows that a combination of ETFs and much of the 100 stocks within QQQ, owned directly here, are the "risk on" asset basket for QQH.

Actually, there is some leverage at play here, since the top holding, nearly 20% of the fund, is in a 3x QQQ ETF.

Finally, zeroing in on QQH's worst period, as we should do to stress test any ETF that aims to manage risk actively, this says it all. From the start of 2022 to 27 months later (3/31/24), QQQ dove and recovered. It netted a 13% return, or about 6% annualized. Not bad considering we had the dawn of a new bond bear market that also hit stocks hard, temporarily.

But the whole idea of managing risk and not just "buying and holding" and hoping the market and time will bail us out is that each new up cycle can start with close to what we had when things turned south. But when you get 40% in the hole as QQH did here, it costs time as much as money. Because even when the money is nearly recovered, the time is lost forever.

QQH: I say no way, for my portfolio

Every investor chooses their own path. For me, since my whole approach is centered around being competitive in any market conditions, QQH is not a value-add for me. I used inverse ETFs (single, double and triple at times), put options and tactical positioning, rather than a secret formula. It takes some work, but that's what the investing group I run is about.

Others might find value here. I just don't see it. And while QQH will do just fine when the Nasdaq 100 appreciates, it is not worth the risk versus alternative approaches. This ETF is far from the only one that doesn't deliver on the idea of true risk management. I've been around that style for decades and the more one tries to "get away with" capturing most of the upside in the good times, the less margin for error they have in unfriendly markets.

So based on my lack of confidence in QQH to play a role in my portfolio's "department of defense," I rate it a sell.
